notes: HEI models Igot as inactive rather than dead because contemporary reporting strongly supports severe operational distress and non-payment complaints, but does not provide a clean official shutdown announcement or terminal operating date. Do not mark as dead without stronger terminal evidence.

summary: Namecoin / Bitcoin exchange operated under the Bitparking name and associated with Chris Double. Public forum records show exchange.bitparking.com operating by 2012, followed by an April 2013 exchange data-drive corruption incident, withdrawal-only recovery efforts, and statements that the exchange would cease operation.
notes: HEI models Bitparking as inactive rather than dead because available sources strongly support exchange operation, drive-corruption failure, withdrawal-only recovery, and cessation language, but the current evidence set is largely forum / community-preserved rather than a clean official shutdown notice. Do not assign a death_reason without stronger terminal evidence.

summary: China-based Bitcoin exchange founded in 2011 and widely described as China's first Bitcoin exchange. In September 2015, BTCChina rebranded to BTCC as the company shifted toward a more global identity.
notes: HEI models this as the historical BTCChina brand record. The 2015-09-15 rebrand to BTCC is used as the terminal brand marker. Later BTCC trading restrictions and ownership changes should be handled separately if a BTCC successor record is modeled.

summary: New Zealand BTC/NZD exchange announced in September 2011. BitNZ later announced it would close because New Zealand banking access made Bitcoin exchange operations impossible, and customers were asked to withdraw funds by 2017-04-15.
notes: HEI models BitNZ as dead / voluntary_shutdown because reporting describes a planned closure driven by banking-access problems rather than a hack or insolvency. The terminal date uses the customer withdrawal deadline of 2017-04-15.

summary: South Korea-based centralized cryptocurrency exchange established in 2017. Coinrail suffered a major June 2018 hack reported at roughly $40 million, later resumed service, but current market-data trackers show no active markets or classify it as inactive / untracked.

summary: France-based BTC/EUR exchange operated by Paymium. Bitcoin-Central launched in December 2010, later announced a regulated banking / payment-institution framework in December 2012, and relaunched in September 2013 after a months-long closure. HEI models it as inactive because available sources mark the historical exchange as discontinued but do not establish a clean terminal shutdown date.

summary: Spain-based open-source decentralized peer-to-peer Bitcoin exchange project. HEI models it as inactive because a definitive shutdown announcement or terminal date is not established.

notes: GLBSE was not a conventional fiat/crypto spot exchange; it is included as a historical Bitcoin-denominated asset / securities exchange. HEI uses regulation as the death_reason because contemporary reporting and community records cite AML, tax, and regulatory concerns as the stated closure rationale.

summary: US-focused bitcoin seller / broker platform launched around November 2012. CoinDesk and Cointelegraph reported in October 2013 that BTCQuick had reached nearly $2 million in bitcoin sales. HEI models BTCQuick as inactive because the current evidence set supports historical operation but does not establish a definitive shutdown announcement or terminal date.

summary: Bitcoin-denominated securities / asset exchange operated by Jon Montroll, also known as Ukyo. notes: BitFunder was not a conventional fiat/crypto spot exchange; it is included as a historical Bitcoin-denominated securities / asset exchange. summary: Non-custodial peer-to-peer cryptocurrency marketplace launched in 2017 as LocalEthereum and rebranded to LocalCryptos in 2019 when it added Bitcoin support. notes: HEI uses regulation as death_reason because contemporary reports preserve BitZ's shutdown schedule and state that the closure followed Chinese regulatory policy requirements. The terminal date uses 2021-10-21, when spot trading and platform login were scheduled to stop.

summary: Centralized cryptocurrency exchange launched around 2017. Current market-data trackers show Crex24 with no active markets, no tracked volume, or as no longer operational. notes: HEI keeps Crex24 inactive rather than dead because public trackers indicate no operational market data / no longer operational status, while stronger official terminal shutdown evidence is not attached. Do not assign death_reason or death_date without stronger closure evidence.

notes: HEI models DragonEX as inactive rather than dead. The March 2019 hack is recorded as a major incident, not a terminal shutdown marker. Do not assign death_reason or death_date without stronger closure evidence.

summary: Australia-based cryptocurrency exchange / service provider founded in 2013. Kraken acquired Bit Trade on 2020-01-14, bringing the Bit Trade team into Kraken Australia and using the acquisition to expand AUD liquidity, spot exchange, and OTC services in the Australian market.
notes: HEI models BitTrade Australia as acquired because Kraken acquired the Australian crypto service provider on 2020-01-14. Later regulatory materials refer to Bit Trade Pty Ltd as the operator/provider of Kraken services in Australia, so this is not a dead exchange record; the terminal date marks the end of the independent Bit Trade exchange/service identity.

summary: Pakistan-based Bitcoin exchange launched in 2014 as one of the country's first local Bitcoin exchange platforms. Urdubit announced it would shut down after the State Bank of Pakistan issued an April 2018 circular barring banks and payment providers from facilitating virtual-currency transactions.
notes: HEI uses regulation as the primary death_reason because Urdubit announced closure immediately after the State Bank of Pakistan's April 2018 prohibition on banks and payment providers facilitating virtual-currency transactions. Launch date follows CoinDesk's 2014-10-11 launch report.

summary: QUOINE-operated crypto-only centralized exchange launched in June 2017 as QRYPTOS. In September 2018, QUOINE merged / relaunched its historical platforms QUOINEX and QRYPTOS into Liquid, making QRYPTOS a predecessor brand rather than an independently active exchange.
notes: HEI uses rebranded because QRYPTOS was not an insolvency or hack shutdown; it was merged/relaunched into Liquid together with QUOINEX. A future Liquid/Liquid by Quoine entity can be linked as successor_id once present in the canonical dataset.

summary: Hong Kong-linked centralized cryptocurrency exchange founded around 2017. summary: UK-linked centralized cryptocurrency exchange launched in May 2017. summary: Peer-to-peer OTC cryptocurrency exchange platform associated with the LocalMonero team. AgoraDesk supported no-KYC P2P Bitcoin trading, while LocalMonero focused on Monero. summary: Cryptocurrency exchange operating under the txbit.io domain. Txbit announced in August 2023 that it would shut down, with services scheduled to end on 2023-09-14. Exchange-tracker references later show the market as inactive or unavailable. HEI treats Txbit as a dead exchange with voluntary_shutdown as the death_reason because the terminal marker is the operator-announced shutdown rather than a hack, acquisition, rebrand, insolvency filing, or regulatory enforcement action.
notes: Launch date is approximate/year-level and should be refined if stronger launch evidence is later added. Death date follows Txbit's announced 2023-09-14 shutdown schedule.

summary: Cryptocurrency exchange that halted withdrawals in June 2022 during a liquidity dispute involving a large counterparty. CoinFLEX later became linked to the Open Exchange / OPNX transition, with OPNX positioned as a new venue connected to CoinFLEX leadership and creditor recovery plans. HEI treats CoinFLEX as a rebranded / superseded exchange identity with OPNX as the successor record, while retaining the 2022 withdrawal halt as the critical failure event.
notes: CoinFLEX is added as a predecessor / superseded identity linked to existing OPNX record. Confidence is medium because public reporting supports the continuity, but the transition is not a simple clean exchange-name rebrand. URL audit 2026-06-18: The original CoinFLEX domain now serves creditor recovery and court-information content rather than the former exchange.
